10 Discrete IVs and Other

Colin Kuehl

July 21, 2026

Set-Up

Packages

library(foreign)
library(ggcorrplot)
## Loading required package: ggplot2
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.2.1     ✔ readr     2.2.0
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ purrr     1.2.2     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(stargazer)
## 
## Please cite as: 
## 
##  Hlavac, Marek (2022). stargazer: Well-Formatted Regression and Summary Statistics Tables.
##  R package version 5.2.3. https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=stargazer

Set your working directory and load the height wage data(HeightWage_MenWomenUS_HW.csv).

setwd("~/Dropbox/POLS 641/CMU Su26/cmucourse26/ClassCode/Day 10")
hw <- read.csv("HeightWage_MenWomenUS_HW.csv")
###Codebook in html - knit now to show in viewer
Variable name Description
male Male (1 = yes, 0 = no)
white White (1 = yes, 0 = no)
black Black (1 = yes, 0 = no)
hispanic Hispanic (1 = yes, 0 = no)
wage96 Adult hourly wages (dollars) reported in 1996 (salary and wages in past calendar year divided by hours worked in past calendar year)
height85 Adult height (inches), self-reported in 1985
height81 Adolescent height (inches), self-reported in 1981
athlets Participation in high school athletics (1 = yes, 0 = no)
clubnum Number of club memberships in high school, excluding athletics, academic/honor society clubs, and vocational clubs
momed79 Mother’s years of education
daded79 Father’s years of education
mompro2 Mother in a professional/managerial occupation (1 = yes, 0 = no)
poppro2 Father in a professional/managerial occupation (1 = yes, 0 = no)
siblings Number of siblings
age Age (years) in 1996
esteem80 Score on Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale as an adolescent in 1980 (higher values indicate higher self-esteem)
hgc96 Highest grade of education completed in 1996
REGION
norest96 Northeast (1 = yes, 0 = no)
norcen96 North Central (1 = yes, 0 = no)
south96 South (1 = yes, 0 = no)
west96 West (1 = yes, 0 = no)

Exploring Wage

Explore the data, we are mostly interested in wage:

summary(hw$wage96)
##     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max.      NAs 
##    0.000    6.743   10.783   14.177   16.213 1533.333     5756
hist(hw$wage96)

summary(lm(wage96 ~ esteem80, data=hw))
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ esteem80, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -17.59   -7.23   -3.17    2.09 1518.09 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  -4.3081     2.8568  -1.508    0.132    
## esteem80      0.7821     0.1199   6.524 7.35e-11 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.58 on 6719 degrees of freedom
##   (5965 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.006295,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.006147 
## F-statistic: 42.56 on 1 and 6719 DF,  p-value: 7.348e-11

Using just continuous variables, create a multivariate model predicting wage. Practice our interpretation.

Dummy Variables

Can add to our multivariate regression

table(hw$white)
## 
##    0    1 
## 5176 7510
table(hw$male)
## 
##    0    1 
## 6283 6403
ck1a <- (lm(wage96~male, data=hw))#Tells us average for males and non-males
summary(ck1a)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ male, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -14.89   -7.20   -3.36    2.01 1519.96 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  13.3761     0.4781  27.975   <2e-16 ***
## male          1.5186     0.6584   2.307   0.0211 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.36 on 6928 degrees of freedom
##   (5756 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.0007673,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.0006231 
## F-statistic:  5.32 on 1 and 6928 DF,  p-value: 0.02111
hw %>% group_by(male) %>% summarise(mean=mean(wage96, na.rm=T))
## # A tibble: 2 × 2
##    male  mean
##   <int> <dbl>
## 1     0  13.4
## 2     1  14.9
13.37+1.51
## [1] 14.88
t.test(hw$wage96[hw$male==1], hw$wage96[hw$male==0], var.equal = FALSE)
## 
##  Welch Two Sample t-test
## 
## data:  hw$wage96[hw$male == 1] and hw$wage96[hw$male == 0]
## t = 2.2344, df = 4778, p-value = 0.02551
## alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.1861617 2.8510406
## sample estimates:
## mean of x mean of y 
##  14.89472  13.37612
plot(hw$male,hw$wage96, ylim=c(0,25)) #Really zooming in. Black lines are just a bunch of dots
abline(h=13.37, col="red")
abline(h=13.37+1.51, col="blue")
abline(abline(ck1a, lty=6, col="purple"))

plot(hw$wage96~jitter(hw$male, 1), ylim=c(0,25)) #jitter will spread out your data a bit. in some cases with super large N I will take a random sample to get a better feel for the data.
abline(h=13.37, col="red")
abline(h=13.37+1.51, col="blue")
abline(abline(ck1a, lty=6, col="purple"))

Indicate dummy on Multivariate Regression plot

ck3 <- lm(wage96~  age + male, data=hw) #Shows differences between males and females
summary(ck3)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ age + male, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -15.18   -7.18   -3.38    2.02 1520.29 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)  
## (Intercept) 11.40502    5.34835   2.132   0.0330 *
## age          0.05299    0.15280   0.347   0.7288  
## male         1.70840    0.68715   2.486   0.0129 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.82 on 6570 degrees of freedom
##   (6113 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.0009517,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.0006476 
## F-statistic: 3.129 on 2 and 6570 DF,  p-value: 0.04381
plot(jitter(hw$age[hw$male==0]),(hw$wage96[hw$male==0]), col="red", pch=1, xlab="Age", ylab="Hourly Wage", main="Age as a Determinent of \n Wage by Gender", ylim=c(0,100)) #plots female  note: \n creats new line 
#Shorten ylim to show difference
points(jitter(hw$age[hw$male==1]), hw$wage96[hw$male==1], col="blue", pch=1) #plots male
legend(50,90, legend=c("Female", "Male"), col=c("red", "blue"), pch=c(1, 16), bty="n")
abline(a=11.405, b=.052, col="red") # intercept and slope from above
abline(a=(11.405+1.708), b=.052, col="blue")

More Multivariate Models

ck2 <- lm(wage96~ male+ height85 + siblings + age, data=hw) #Shows differences between males and females
summary(ck2)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ male + height85 + siblings + age, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -18.65   -7.07   -3.18    2.16 1518.22 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) -8.13697    9.57694  -0.850  0.39556    
## male        -0.27730    1.00588  -0.276  0.78281    
## height85     0.32013    0.12279   2.607  0.00915 ** 
## siblings    -0.68540    0.13512  -5.072 4.04e-07 ***
## age          0.09949    0.15667   0.635  0.52546    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 28.06 on 6377 degrees of freedom
##   (6304 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.006359,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.005736 
## F-statistic:  10.2 on 4 and 6377 DF,  p-value: 3.116e-08
ck2a <- lm(wage96~ male+ height85 + siblings + age, data=hw)
summary(ck2a)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ male + height85 + siblings + age, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -18.65   -7.07   -3.18    2.16 1518.22 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) -8.13697    9.57694  -0.850  0.39556    
## male        -0.27730    1.00588  -0.276  0.78281    
## height85     0.32013    0.12279   2.607  0.00915 ** 
## siblings    -0.68540    0.13512  -5.072 4.04e-07 ***
## age          0.09949    0.15667   0.635  0.52546    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 28.06 on 6377 degrees of freedom
##   (6304 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.006359,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.005736 
## F-statistic:  10.2 on 4 and 6377 DF,  p-value: 3.116e-08

Question: Does participation in high school athletics(athlets) have an affect on wage?

summary(lm(wage96~athlets, data=hw))
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ athlets, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -16.42   -7.19   -3.19    2.18 1516.92 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  12.6851     0.4391  28.889  < 2e-16 ***
## athlets       3.7300     0.6795   5.489 4.18e-08 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.58 on 6773 degrees of freedom
##   (5911 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.004429,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.004282 
## F-statistic: 30.13 on 1 and 6773 DF,  p-value: 4.184e-08
summary(lm(wage96~athlets+hgc96+siblings+height85+white+clubnum, data=hw))
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ athlets + hgc96 + siblings + height85 + 
##     white + clubnum, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -27.39   -6.59   -2.72    2.08 1517.47 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) -19.64573    6.01271  -3.267  0.00109 ** 
## athlets       1.42530    0.72873   1.956  0.05052 .  
## hgc96         1.52887    0.15831   9.657  < 2e-16 ***
## siblings     -0.26847    0.13814  -1.943  0.05200 .  
## height85      0.20124    0.08462   2.378  0.01743 *  
## white         1.28600    0.71669   1.794  0.07280 .  
## clubnum      -0.32490    0.36239  -0.897  0.37000    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.53 on 6623 degrees of freedom
##   (6056 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.02465,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.02376 
## F-statistic: 27.89 on 6 and 6623 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Categorical Variables

#Use region variables, these have already been converted into dummy variables
reg1 <-lm(wage96~norest96+norcen96+west96+south96, data=hw) #why is south NA?  . . .. perfect multicollinearity. We need a reference category
summary(reg1)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ norest96 + norcen96 + west96 + south96, 
##     data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -17.04   -7.06   -3.21    2.08 1519.03 
## 
## Coefficients: (1 not defined because of singularities)
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  12.3480     0.5189  23.795  < 2e-16 ***
## norest96      4.6967     0.9733   4.825 1.43e-06 ***
## norcen96      1.9525     0.8514   2.293   0.0219 *  
## west96        2.9500     0.9106   3.240   0.0012 ** 
## south96           NA         NA      NA       NA    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.36 on 6860 degrees of freedom
##   (5822 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.003894,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.003459 
## F-statistic: 8.939 on 3 and 6860 DF,  p-value: 6.585e-06
reg2 <-lm(wage96~norcen96+west96+south96, data=hw) #Take south out to compare to 
summary(reg2)#remember we interpret these coefficients as the difference between each region and the reference category. 
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ norcen96 + west96 + south96, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -17.04   -7.06   -3.21    2.08 1519.03 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  17.0447     0.8235  20.699  < 2e-16 ***
## norcen96     -2.7441     1.0648  -2.577  0.00998 ** 
## west96       -1.7467     1.1127  -1.570  0.11651    
## south96      -4.6967     0.9733  -4.825 1.43e-06 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.36 on 6860 degrees of freedom
##   (5822 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.003894,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.003459 
## F-statistic: 8.939 on 3 and 6860 DF,  p-value: 6.585e-06
reg3 <-lm(wage96~hgc96+norest96+norcen96+west96, data=hw) #add in education
summary(reg3)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ hgc96 + norest96 + norcen96 + west96, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -25.99   -6.56   -2.64    2.14 1517.88 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  -9.0733     1.8462  -4.915 9.10e-07 ***
## hgc96         1.6364     0.1355  12.080  < 2e-16 ***
## norest96      3.9749     0.9652   4.118 3.86e-05 ***
## norcen96      1.6173     0.8435   1.917  0.05523 .  
## west96        2.7397     0.9014   3.039  0.00238 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.08 on 6857 degrees of freedom
##   (5824 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.02465,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.02408 
## F-statistic: 43.33 on 4 and 6857 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
plot(hw$hgc96, hw$wage96, xlab="Education level", ylab="Hourly Wage", main="Education as a Determinent of \n Wage by Region", ylim=c(0,100))
abline(a=-9.073, b=1.634, col="red") # The South
abline(a=(-9.073+3.97), b=1.634, col="blue") #Northeast
abline(a=(-9.073+1.613), b=1.634, col="hotpink") #NorthCentral
abline(a=(-9.073+2.739), b=1.634, col="green") #West
legend(0,95, legend=c("South", "Northeast", "North Central", "West"), col=c("red", "blue", "hotpink", "green"),lty=1, bty="n")

#Racial category
cat1 <- lm(wage96~ black + hispanic, data=hw) #Wage difference compared to white
summary(cat1)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ black + hispanic, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -15.64   -7.18   -3.28    2.12 1521.40 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  15.6363     0.4500  34.749  < 2e-16 ***
## black        -3.6989     0.7640  -4.842 1.32e-06 ***
## hispanic     -2.2342     0.8857  -2.522   0.0117 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.33 on 6927 degrees of freedom
##   (5756 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.003554,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.003267 
## F-statistic: 12.35 on 2 and 6927 DF,  p-value: 4.408e-06
sink <- lm(wage96~ male+ norcen96+west96+south96+ black + hispanic, data=hw)
summary(sink)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ male + norcen96 + west96 + south96 + black + 
##     hispanic, data = hw)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -18.81   -6.94   -3.11    2.06 1522.02 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  17.5477     0.9399  18.671  < 2e-16 ***
## male          1.2627     0.6611   1.910  0.05617 .  
## norcen96     -3.1336     1.0690  -2.931  0.00339 ** 
## west96       -1.4993     1.1364  -1.319  0.18710    
## south96      -4.1520     0.9820  -4.228 2.39e-05 ***
## black        -3.1006     0.7921  -3.914 9.15e-05 ***
## hispanic     -2.5418     0.9387  -2.708  0.00679 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.32 on 6857 degrees of freedom
##   (5822 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.007076,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.006207 
## F-statistic: 8.144 on 6 and 6857 DF,  p-value: 8.541e-09

Summarizing regression models

#Yesterday we used model summary, today we will use stargazer to create nice regression tables
library("stargazer")
stargazer(ck1a,reg3, sink, type="text", title = "Multivariate Regression Models")
## 
## Multivariate Regression Models
## ===========================================================================================
##                                               Dependent variable:                          
##                     -----------------------------------------------------------------------
##                                                     wage96                                 
##                              (1)                     (2)                      (3)          
## -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## male                       1.519**                                          1.263*         
##                            (0.658)                                          (0.661)        
##                                                                                            
## hgc96                                              1.636***                                
##                                                    (0.135)                                 
##                                                                                            
## norest96                                           3.975***                                
##                                                    (0.965)                                 
##                                                                                            
## norcen96                                            1.617*                 -3.134***       
##                                                    (0.843)                  (1.069)        
##                                                                                            
## west96                                             2.740***                 -1.499         
##                                                    (0.901)                  (1.136)        
##                                                                                            
## south96                                                                    -4.152***       
##                                                                             (0.982)        
##                                                                                            
## black                                                                      -3.101***       
##                                                                             (0.792)        
##                                                                                            
## hispanic                                                                   -2.542***       
##                                                                             (0.939)        
##                                                                                            
## Constant                  13.376***               -9.073***                17.548***       
##                            (0.478)                 (1.846)                  (0.940)        
##                                                                                            
## -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Observations                6,930                   6,862                    6,864         
## R2                          0.001                   0.025                    0.007         
## Adjusted R2                 0.001                   0.024                    0.006         
## Residual Std. Error   27.363 (df = 6928)      27.080 (df = 6857)      27.323 (df = 6857)   
## F Statistic         5.320** (df = 1; 6928) 43.326*** (df = 4; 6857) 8.144*** (df = 6; 6857)
## ===========================================================================================
## Note:                                                           *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01
#We can also use the coefplot package. 
library(coefplot)#New Package. Will need to install. Also makes coefficient plots

coefplot(ck1a)#point is equal to coefficient. Lines are confidence interval. When CI does not overlap 0 coefficient is significant
## `height` was translated to `width`.
## `height` was translated to `width`.

coefplot(reg2)
## `height` was translated to `width`.
## `height` was translated to `width`.

coefplot(sink, intercept = FALSE, newNames=c(hispanic="Hispanic", black="Black", south96="South", west96="West", norcen96="North Central", male="Male"), title="Predictors of Wage \n 1996", xlab="")
## `height` was translated to `width`.
## `height` was translated to `width`.

multiplot(sink, reg2, intercept=FALSE)
## `height` was translated to `width`.
## `height` was translated to `width`.

coefplot(ck2, intercept = FALSE)#remember comparing size of effect only makes sense when range is the same. 
## `height` was translated to `width`.
## `height` was translated to `width`.

Bonus: Based on your extensive knowledge of American wages and statistics create your version of the best regression model(or models) Double Bonus: Create a table using stargazer

Lets play a bit more with regression assumptions and transforming variables

# Restrict to employed respondents with complete height data
hwdata2 <- subset(hw, !is.na(wage96) & wage96 > 0 & !is.na(height85))

hwdata2$lwage <- log(hwdata2$wage96)   # log hourly wage

Why Log Wages?

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(hwdata2$wage96, main = "Raw Wage",      xlab = "Hourly Wage (USD)")
hist(hwdata2$lwage,  main = "log(Wage)",     xlab = "Log Hourly Wage")

  • Raw wages are right-skewed — a few very high earners pull the tail
  • Log transformation compresses the scale and produces an approximately normal distribution
  • Interpretation shifts: a 1-inch increase in height is associated with a percentage change in wages

Scatter Plot: Height and Log Wages

plot(hwdata2$lwage ~ hwdata2$height85,
     xlab = "Height in 1985 (inches)",
     ylab = "Log Hourly Wage (1996)")

abline(lm(lwage ~ height85, data = hwdata2), col = "red")

  • Is there a positive relationship? Does it look roughly linear?
  • Outliers at the extremes are worth noting

Compare: Logged vs. Unlogged Outcome

model.wage.orig <- lm(wage96 ~ height85, data = hwdata2)
model.wage      <- lm(lwage  ~ height85, data = hwdata2)

summary(model.wage.orig)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = wage96 ~ height85, data = hwdata2)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
##  -17.16   -7.28   -3.56    1.83 1520.13 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  -7.2833     5.7076  -1.276 0.201975    
## height85      0.3252     0.0846   3.845 0.000122 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 27.96 on 6527 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.00226,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.002107 
## F-statistic: 14.78 on 1 and 6527 DF,  p-value: 0.0001218
summary(model.wage)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = lwage ~ height85, data = hwdata2)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -5.0840 -0.3802  0.0322  0.4337  5.1186 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 0.102451   0.156177   0.656    0.512    
## height85    0.033557   0.002315  14.497   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7651 on 6527 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.03119,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.03104 
## F-statistic: 210.2 on 1 and 6527 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
  • Compare \(R^2\), residual standard error, and residual patterns
  • Logged model: coefficient interpreted as “a 1-inch increase in height is associated with a \(\hat{\beta} \times 100\%\) change in wages”

Regression Diagnostics

The Four Diagnostic Plots

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model.wage, labels.id = rownames(hwdata2))

Four plots, four questions:

  1. Residuals vs. Fitted — is the relationship linear? Should hug the dashed line
  2. Normal Q-Q — are errors normally distributed? Points should follow the diagonal
  3. Scale-Location — is variance constant (homoskedasticity)? Should be flat, no V-shape
  4. Residuals vs. Leverage — are any observations disproportionately influential? (Cook’s distance)

Reading Each Plot

Residuals vs. Fitted

  • Curve or U-shape → non-linearity (try a transformation)
  • Fan shape → heteroskedasticity (use robust SE)
  • Should be random scatter around zero

Residuals vs. Leverage

  • Points outside Cook’s distance contour are high-influence
  • High leverage \(\neq\) bad, but worth inspecting
  • Ask: does the result change substantially if I drop this case?

Removing Outliers — With Caution

# Identify high-wage outliers (e.g., top 1%)
wage_cutoff <- quantile(hwdata2$wage96, 0.99, na.rm = TRUE)
hwdata4 <- subset(hwdata2, wage96 < wage_cutoff)

model2.wage <- lm(lwage ~ height85, data = hwdata4)
summary(model2.wage)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = lwage ~ height85, data = hwdata4)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -5.0566 -0.3589  0.0508  0.4435  2.0753 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 0.069746   0.148190   0.471    0.638    
## height85    0.033639   0.002197  15.314   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.722 on 6453 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.03507,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.03492 
## F-statistic: 234.5 on 1 and 6453 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model2.wage, labels.id = rownames(hwdata4))

Warning: Only remove observations if you have a substantive theoretical reason. Removing outliers to improve fit is data manipulation. Here we may want to keep high earners — they are real data points.

Part 3: Multicollinearity

A Richer Model

# Predict log wages with height plus controls
model6 <- lm(lwage ~ height85 + hgc96 + male + daded79 + momed79,
             data = hwdata2)
summary(model6)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = lwage ~ height85 + hgc96 + male + daded79 + momed79, 
##     data = hwdata2)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -4.8710 -0.3362  0.0406  0.3829  5.0215 
## 
## Coefficients:
##               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) -2.078e-02  2.203e-01  -0.094 0.924846    
## height85     1.184e-02  3.457e-03   3.425 0.000619 ***
## hgc96        9.843e-02  4.393e-03  22.408  < 2e-16 ***
## male         1.934e-01  2.790e-02   6.932 4.64e-12 ***
## daded79      1.745e-02  3.319e-03   5.258 1.51e-07 ***
## momed79      8.507e-05  4.151e-03   0.020 0.983651    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7093 on 5477 degrees of freedom
##   (1046 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1551, Adjusted R-squared:  0.1543 
## F-statistic: 201.1 on 5 and 5477 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
  • Unit of analysis: individual respondent
  • Are father’s education (daded79) and mother’s education (momed79) correlated with each other?

The Problem: Correlated Predictors

plot(hwdata2$daded79, hwdata2$momed79,
     xlab = "Father's Education (years)",
     ylab = "Mother's Education (years)")

cor(hwdata2[, c("daded79", "momed79")], use = "complete")
##           daded79   momed79
## daded79 1.0000000 0.6583598
## momed79 0.6583598 1.0000000
  • Parents’ education levels tend to be correlated — assortative mating
  • Putting both in the same model asks OLS to separate effects it cannot easily distinguish

Fit the Model and Check VIF

library(car)
## Loading required package: carData
## 
## Attaching package: 'car'
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     recode
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     some
vif(model6)
## height85    hgc96     male  daded79  momed79 
## 2.166372 1.232115 2.116669 1.836237 1.861024
  • VIF \(>\) 5: warrants inspection
  • VIF \(>\) 10: serious problem — coefficients are unreliable
  • Note how individual coefficients may be insignificant even when the overall model fits well

Inspect the Diagnostics

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model6, labels.id = rownames(hwdata2))

Also compare single-predictor models to the joint model:

summary(lm(lwage ~ daded79, data = hwdata2))
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = lwage ~ daded79, data = hwdata2)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -5.0589 -0.3746  0.0480  0.4198  4.9039 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 1.912970   0.030149   63.45   <2e-16 ***
## daded79     0.043197   0.002564   16.85   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7547 on 5657 degrees of freedom
##   (870 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.04778,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.04761 
## F-statistic: 283.8 on 1 and 5657 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
summary(lm(lwage ~ momed79, data = hwdata2))
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = lwage ~ momed79, data = hwdata2)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -5.1732 -0.3791  0.0466  0.4284  4.9159 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 1.861677   0.034598   53.81   <2e-16 ***
## momed79     0.046469   0.003035   15.31   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7611 on 6154 degrees of freedom
##   (373 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.03671,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.03655 
## F-statistic: 234.5 on 1 and 6154 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

If each predictor is significant alone but not together, that is a classic multicollinearity signature.

Some Experiments Basics

Set working directory and unpack packages

#install.packages("pwr")
#install.packages("AER")
library(haven)
library(tidyverse)
library(pwr)
library(AER)
## Loading required package: lmtest
## Loading required package: zoo
## 
## Attaching package: 'zoo'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     as.Date, as.Date.numeric
## Loading required package: sandwich
## Loading required package: survival
#More info here: https://www.statmethods.net/stats/power.html

Recall, with experiments we’re focused more on design than statistical analysis.

#Practice data from the textbook.

GGD <- read_csv("GerberGreenData.csv")
## Rows: 29381 Columns: 5
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## dbl (5): PeopleHH, Ward, ContactAssigned, Voted, ContactObserved
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
summary(GGD)
##     PeopleHH          Ward       ContactAssigned      Voted       
##  Min.   :1.000   Min.   : 2.00   Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :0.0000  
##  1st Qu.:1.000   1st Qu.:10.00   1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:0.0000  
##  Median :1.000   Median :17.00   Median :0.0000   Median :0.0000  
##  Mean   :1.497   Mean   :16.43   Mean   :0.1972   Mean   :0.4525  
##  3rd Qu.:2.000   3rd Qu.:24.00   3rd Qu.:0.0000   3rd Qu.:1.0000  
##  Max.   :2.000   Max.   :30.00   Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :1.0000  
##  NAs    :1       NAs    :1       NAs    :1        NAs    :1       
##  ContactObserved  
##  Min.   :0.00000  
##  1st Qu.:0.00000  
##  Median :0.00000  
##  Mean   :0.05497  
##  3rd Qu.:0.00000  
##  Max.   :1.00000  
## 
#check balance across wards
b1 <- table(GGD$Ward, GGD$ContactAssigned)
prop.table(b1, 1) #Balance looks good. 
##     
##              0         1
##   2  0.8016148 0.1983852
##   3  0.8619120 0.1380880
##   4  0.8193146 0.1806854
##   5  0.8094406 0.1905594
##   6  0.8006873 0.1993127
##   7  0.8047404 0.1952596
##   8  0.7810362 0.2189638
##   9  0.7970123 0.2029877
##   10 0.8086614 0.1913386
##   11 0.7965839 0.2034161
##   12 0.7794643 0.2205357
##   13 0.8108327 0.1891673
##   14 0.8070352 0.1929648
##   15 0.7778915 0.2221085
##   16 0.7826087 0.2173913
##   17 0.7720430 0.2279570
##   18 0.8095559 0.1904441
##   19 0.8295964 0.1704036
##   20 0.7973568 0.2026432
##   21 0.8220339 0.1779661
##   22 0.8129117 0.1870883
##   23 0.8051471 0.1948529
##   24 0.8012259 0.1987741
##   25 0.7930299 0.2069701
##   26 0.8176471 0.1823529
##   27 0.8182471 0.1817529
##   28 0.8009050 0.1990950
##   29 0.7974217 0.2025783
##   30 0.7973734 0.2026266
summary(lm(GGD$ContactAssigned~GGD$Ward)) # Can run T-test or regression to see if sig dif. Ward is not correlated with treatment assignment
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = GGD$ContactAssigned ~ GGD$Ward)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.1973 -0.1972 -0.1972 -0.1971  0.8029 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 1.971e-01  5.285e-03  37.293   <2e-16 ***
## GGD$Ward    6.431e-06  2.890e-04   0.022    0.982    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.3979 on 29378 degrees of freedom
##   (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  1.686e-08,  Adjusted R-squared:  -3.402e-05 
## F-statistic: 0.0004952 on 1 and 29378 DF,  p-value: 0.9822
#across # of people in household
b2 <- table(GGD$PeopleHH, GGD$ContactAssigned)
prop.table(b2, 1)
##    
##             0         1
##   1 0.7999188 0.2000812
##   2 0.8056963 0.1943037
#use dplyr
balance <- GGD %>% group_by(ContactAssigned)%>% summarise(People=mean(PeopleHH), Ward=mean(Ward))
t(balance)
##                     [,1]      [,2] [,3]
## ContactAssigned  0.00000  1.000000   NA
## People           1.49894  1.489817   NA
## Ward            16.43110 16.433725   NA
#Experimental results: Does talking to people increase likelihood of voting? 

#ITT does being assigned to treatment actually effect the likilood of voting(regardless of compliance)?
ITT <-(lm(GGD$Voted~GGD$ContactAssigned))
summary(ITT)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = GGD$Voted ~ GGD$ContactAssigned)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.4720 -0.4477 -0.4477  0.5523  0.5523 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         0.447681   0.003240 138.154  < 2e-16 ***
## GGD$ContactAssigned 0.024359   0.007297   3.338 0.000844 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.4977 on 29378 degrees of freedom
##   (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.0003792,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.0003452 
## F-statistic: 11.14 on 1 and 29378 DF,  p-value: 0.0008441
#Voting is dummy variable and this is experimental. When we use OLS on a dummy we call it a linear probablity model and it is interpreted as percentage change in Y from a one unit change in X. An advanced class would talk about the advantages of LPM vs logit or probit for binary DVs.  

#What variables are we missing from our model?

summary(lm(Voted ~ ContactAssigned+Ward+PeopleHH, data=GGD))#adding controls does not change coefficient, only signifigance
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Voted ~ ContactAssigned + Ward + PeopleHH, data = GGD)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.5742 -0.4492 -0.3618  0.5205  0.6565 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)     0.233020   0.010453  22.292  < 2e-16 ***
## ContactAssigned 0.025291   0.007240   3.493 0.000478 ***
## Ward            0.003655   0.000361  10.125  < 2e-16 ***
## PeopleHH        0.103146   0.005800  17.784  < 2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.4938 on 29376 degrees of freedom
##   (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.016,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.0159 
## F-statistic: 159.2 on 3 and 29376 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
#Compliance: 
  
#ATE model
ATE <-(lm(GGD$Voted~GGD$ContactObserved)) #The effect of actually being treated
summary(ATE)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = GGD$Voted ~ GGD$ContactObserved)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.5901 -0.4445 -0.4445  0.5555  0.5555 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         0.444480   0.002981  149.13   <2e-16 ***
## GGD$ContactObserved 0.145612   0.012713   11.45   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.4966 on 29378 degrees of freedom
##   (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.004446,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.004412 
## F-statistic: 131.2 on 1 and 29378 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
#percentage of those assigned to treatment actually treated
summary(lm(ContactObserved ~ ContactAssigned, data=GGD))#table 10.2 in book. 
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = ContactObserved ~ ContactAssigned, data = GGD)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.2787  0.0000  0.0000  0.0000  0.7213 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                   Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)     -2.614e-14  1.297e-03    0.00        1    
## ContactAssigned  2.787e-01  2.920e-03   95.47   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.1991 on 29378 degrees of freedom
##   (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.2368, Adjusted R-squared:  0.2368 
## F-statistic:  9114 on 1 and 29378 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
twosls <- ivreg(Voted ~ ContactObserved | ContactAssigned, data=GGD) 
summary(twosls)
## 
## Call:
## ivreg(formula = Voted ~ ContactObserved | ContactAssigned, data = GGD)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.5351 -0.4477 -0.4477  0.5523  0.5523 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)     0.447681   0.003235 138.386  < 2e-16 ***
## ContactObserved 0.087392   0.026135   3.344 0.000827 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.4968 on 29378 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-Squared: 0.003735,    Adjusted R-squared: 0.003701 
## Wald test: 11.18 on 1 and 29378 DF,  p-value: 0.0008272
stargazer(ATE,ITT, twosls,  type="text", title = "Comparison of Experimental Models")
## 
## Comparison of Experimental Models
## ==================================================================
##                                         Dependent variable:       
##                                  ---------------------------------
##                                         Voted            Voted    
##                                          OLS          instrumental
##                                                         variable  
##                                     (1)        (2)        (3)     
## ------------------------------------------------------------------
## ContactObserved                   0.146***                        
##                                   (0.013)                         
##                                                                   
## ContactAssigned                             0.024***              
##                                              (0.007)              
##                                                                   
## ContactObserved                                         0.087***  
##                                                         (0.026)   
##                                                                   
## Constant                          0.444***  0.448***    0.448***  
##                                   (0.003)    (0.003)    (0.003)   
##                                                                   
## ------------------------------------------------------------------
## Observations                       29,380    29,380      29,380   
## R2                                 0.004     0.0004      0.004    
## Adjusted R2                        0.004     0.0003      0.004    
## Residual Std. Error (df = 29378)   0.497      0.498      0.497    
## F Statistic (df = 1; 29378)      131.196*** 11.144***             
## ==================================================================
## Note:                                  *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01
multiplot(ATE,ITT, twosls, title = "Comparison of Experimental Models", xlab="Effect Size(Percentage)", intercept=FALSE)
## `height` was translated to `width`.
## `height` was translated to `width`.

Heterogeneous Treatment Effects

#We have limited data, but say we were interested in if the effect of contact was dependent on the number of people in the household

hhe <- (lm(Voted~ContactObserved*PeopleHH, data=GGD))
summary(hhe)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Voted ~ ContactObserved * PeopleHH, data = GGD)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.6317 -0.5003 -0.3894  0.4997  0.6106 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                           Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)               0.278592   0.009349  29.800  < 2e-16 ***
## ContactObserved           0.184040   0.040132   4.586 4.54e-06 ***
## PeopleHH                  0.110849   0.005925  18.708  < 2e-16 ***
## ContactObserved:PeopleHH -0.026311   0.025274  -1.041    0.298    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.4936 on 29376 degrees of freedom
##   (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.01656,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.01646 
## F-statistic: 164.9 on 3 and 29376 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

I haven’t used it much, but DeclareDesign has good stuff on thinking through experimental designs (https://declaredesign.org/r/declaredesign/)
